items.q: What is hexadecimal A in denary?
items.why: Students forget hex uses A-F to represent 10-15 because we need 16 distinct symbols. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

items.q: What is the denary value of binary 10110?
items.why: Students forget to apply place values. Each position is a power of 2 from right (1, 2, 4, 8, 16...). Reading binary as denary is the most common error.

items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: Which keyword needs both sides true?
items.why: It's and. “or” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: False. “True” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many results can a boolean expression have?
items.why: Exact answer: 2. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
